texto: Jesus’ incarnation and ministry thus present us with the final critique of strategic religion; on the cross, where we see God almost deliberately ‘lose’ – as if duped into being strung up by a scheming, fearful group of clerics – we see the end of power games. God will not play. I sincerely believe that if the Church allows itself to be tied up in strategies, into ‘winning’ people for Christ, it will end inexorably moving towards power-politics, towards support for wars, and away from genuine concern for the ‘other’.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Kester Brewin: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de esforço, persistência e resultados. O trecho “Jesus’ incarnation and ministry thus present us with the final critique of strategic religion; on…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que fe, cross, incarnation pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de termino e incarnation.

I sincerely believe that if the Church allows itself to be tied up in strategies, into ‘winning’ people for Christ, it will end inexorably moving towards power-politics, towards support for wars, and away from genuine concern for the ‘other’.

texto: Jesus’ command to love our neighbours is a command to love all strangers precisely because we too have been strangers to God and welcomed in.
explicacao: Citação de Kester Brewin sobre vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. “Jesus’ command to love our neighbours is a command to love all strangers precisely because we too…” articula amor, acceptance, kester-brewin em registro de amor, com relevância para contextos como kester-brewin e love.
